agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "Evolution of the Shipment aggregate from a 1:1 Shipment-Recipient relationship to a 1:N relationship. This is a fundamental change to the core domain model of a delivery platform, affecting the Shipment aggregate root boundary, all downstream processes (routing, delivery, notification, billing), and external integration contracts. With 2M shipments in production and partner API dependencies, this is a high-risk model migration.",
    "bounded_context": "Shipment Management / Order Fulfillment",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Shipment", "definition": "The aggregate root representing a physical package or consignment moving through the delivery network. Previously owned exactly one Recipient; now owns an ordered collection of one or more Recipients." },
      { "term": "Recipient", "definition": "A value object (or entity, if individually trackable) representing a person or organization receiving a delivery, including name, address, phone number, and now delivery preferences. Transitions from being embedded in Shipment to being a first-class child entity within the Shipment aggregate." },
      { "term": "Delivery Preferences", "definition": "Per-recipient configuration such as preferred delivery window, safe-place instructions, signature requirement, or leave-with-neighbor authorization. New concept introduced with this change." },
      { "term": "Primary Recipient", "definition": "The first or designated main recipient on a multi-recipient shipment. Critical for backward compatibility and for processes that require a single point of contact (e.g., failed delivery escalation)." },
      { "term": "Delivery Stop", "definition": "An individual delivery attempt to a single Recipient's address within a multi-recipient Shipment. This concept may not yet exist in the codebase but becomes necessary when a single shipment has multiple physical delivery locations." },
      { "term": "Split Shipment", "definition": "A potential operational pattern where a multi-recipient shipment is physically divided into sub-parcels at a hub. Must be distinguished from a single parcel with multiple delivery stops." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "A Shipment must have at least one Recipient at all times. Zero-recipient shipments are invalid.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A shipment without a recipient has no destination and cannot enter the delivery network. This is a physical constraint — a parcel must go somewhere.",
      "testable_assertion": "shipment.recipients.count >= 1 must hold true at every state transition of the Shipment lifecycle."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Each Recipient within a Shipment must have a valid name, address, and phone number. Delivery preferences are optional and default to standard delivery if unspecified.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Name, address, and phone are the minimum data required to execute a delivery and contact the recipient on failure. Without these, the delivery is unroutable.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every recipient R in shipment.recipients: R.name is non-blank, R.address passes address validation, R.phone passes phone validation."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "Recipient addresses within a single Shipment must be distinct. A shipment cannot list the same address twice.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a data quality policy to prevent duplicates. A business decision could allow same-address recipients in edge cases (e.g., two people at the same office).",
      "testable_assertion": "No two recipients in shipment.recipients share the same normalized address, unless explicitly overridden."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "Every multi-recipient Shipment must designate exactly one Primary Recipient for backward compatibility, escalation, and billing purposes.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Many downstream systems (notifications, billing, partner APIs, failed-delivery workflows) require a single canonical recipient. Without a primary, these processes break or become ambiguous.",
      "testable_assertion": "shipment.recipients.count(r => r.isPrimary) == 1 for all shipments."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "All existing single-recipient shipments must be treated as having one recipient who is also the Primary Recipient. Migration must be lossless — no existing recipient data may be dropped or altered.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Data integrity for 2M production records. Lossy migration would corrupt delivery history, break audit trails, and violate data retention obligations.",
      "testable_assertion": "Post-migration: for every pre-existing shipment, recipients.count == 1 AND recipients[0].isPrimary == true AND recipients[0].name/address/phone match original values exactly."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "Shipment status must reflect the aggregate delivery state across all recipients. A Shipment is 'Delivered' only when ALL recipients have received their delivery.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Partial delivery of a multi-recipient shipment is not full delivery. Prematurely marking a shipment as delivered breaks SLA tracking, billing, and customer trust.",
      "testable_assertion": "shipment.status == 'Delivered' if and only if all recipients have deliveryStatus == 'Delivered'."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "External partner APIs must continue to receive shipment data in the current single-recipient format until partners have migrated. The Primary Recipient is used as the canonical recipient in legacy API responses.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a compatibility policy, not a physical constraint. It can be relaxed per-partner as they adopt the new API version.",
      "testable_assertion": "Legacy API v1 responses for any shipment return exactly one recipient object, populated from the Primary Recipient."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "Delivery preferences are scoped per-recipient, not per-shipment. Two recipients on the same shipment may have conflicting preferences (e.g., one requires signature, one allows safe-place).",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Delivery preferences represent real-world instructions for each physical delivery stop. Merging them at the shipment level would produce incorrect driver instructions.",
      "testable_assertion": "Each recipient independently owns its delivery preferences; changing one recipient's preferences does not affect another's."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A new shipment is being created",
      "when": "The creator provides two or more recipients with valid name, address, phone, and optional delivery preferences",
      "then": "The shipment is created with all recipients persisted, one designated as Primary, and the shipment status is initialized correctly"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "An existing single-recipient shipment created before the migration",
      "when": "The shipment is retrieved via any API (internal or external)",
      "then": "It returns exactly one recipient marked as Primary, with all original data intact and delivery preferences defaulted to standard"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "A multi-recipient shipment where recipient A has been delivered but recipient B has not",
      "when": "The shipment status is queried",
      "then": "The shipment status is 'Partially Delivered' (or equivalent), NOT 'Delivered'"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "A multi-recipient shipment exists",
      "when": "An external partner queries the shipment via the legacy v1 API",
      "then": "The response contains a single recipient object populated from the Primary Recipient, with no breaking schema changes"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "A shipment is being created with zero recipients",
      "when": "The creation request is submitted",
      "then": "The request is rejected with a validation error; no shipment is persisted"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "A multi-recipient shipment with recipients at different addresses",
      "when": "The shipment is dispatched for delivery",
      "then": "Each recipient generates a distinct delivery stop with its own delivery preferences, and the driver's route includes all stops"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "The data migration has completed for all 2M existing shipments",
      "when": "A reconciliation check is run",
      "then": "Every migrated shipment has exactly one Primary Recipient, and zero data fields have been lost or altered compared to pre-migration snapshots"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-8",
      "given": "A multi-recipient shipment exists",
      "when": "A user attempts to remove the last remaining recipient",
      "then": "The operation is rejected — the invariant of at least one recipient is enforced"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "All recipients on a multi-recipient shipment share the same address (e.g., different departments in one building)", "expected_behavior": "System should allow this if names differ, but flag for review if address AND name are identical. Single delivery stop is generated, but per-recipient preferences still apply independently." },
    { "scenario": "Primary Recipient is removed from a multi-recipient shipment", "expected_behavior": "System must automatically re-designate another recipient as Primary before persisting, or reject the removal. A shipment must never be in a state with recipients but no Primary." },
    { "scenario": "One recipient on a multi-recipient shipment has an undeliverable address", "expected_behavior": "That specific recipient's delivery fails; the shipment enters 'Partially Delivered' or 'Partial Failure' state. Other recipients' deliveries proceed independently." },
    { "scenario": "A partner API webhook fires on shipment status change, but the shipment is multi-recipient", "expected_behavior": "Legacy webhooks send Primary Recipient data only. New v2 webhooks (when available) send full recipient list. Partners must not receive unexpected schema changes." },
    { "scenario": "A shipment has 50+ recipients (bulk/corporate distribution)", "expected_behavior": "System should enforce a reasonable upper bound on recipients per shipment to prevent abuse and operational complexity. Exact limit is a business policy decision (suggest: configurable, default 20)." },
    { "scenario": "Concurrent updates: two agents modify different recipients on the same shipment simultaneously", "expected_behavior": "Optimistic concurrency on the Shipment aggregate prevents lost updates. The second write must fail and retry with fresh data." },
    { "scenario": "Billing: how is a multi-recipient shipment priced?", "expected_behavior": "This must be explicitly defined. Is it one shipment fee + per-stop surcharge? Per-recipient pricing? This is a critical business decision that affects revenue and must be resolved before implementation." },
    { "scenario": "Existing reports and analytics that aggregate by shipment assume 1:1 with recipient", "expected_behavior": "Reports must be audited and updated. Metrics like 'deliveries per day' now differ from 'shipments per day'. Historical data should remain accurate (1 shipment = 1 delivery for old records)." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "HIGH. Multi-recipient shipments introduce a new pricing model question (per-stop surcharge vs flat rate). If not addressed, revenue leakage occurs — a 5-recipient shipment billed as a single delivery loses 4x the delivery cost. Conversely, this feature opens new revenue streams: corporate distribution, gift-to-multiple, and relay logistics. Migration costs are significant (schema migration of 2M records, API versioning, partner coordination).",
    "operational": "HIGH. Route optimization algorithms must be updated — a single shipment now generates multiple delivery stops, fundamentally changing driver workload calculations, vehicle capacity planning, and SLA tracking. The 'Partially Delivered' state is new and requires updated ops dashboards, exception handling workflows, and customer service scripts. Delivery proof (POD) must now be collected per-recipient, not per-shipment.",
    "user": "MEDIUM-HIGH. Customers gain a powerful new capability (ship to multiple people in one order). However, the UX must clearly communicate per-recipient status, and customer service must handle partial-delivery inquiries. Confusion risk is high if status reporting is not granular.",
    "risk": "HIGH. Data migration of 2M records is the single largest risk — any corruption breaks active deliveries. Partner API backward compatibility is the second risk — breaking partner integrations causes immediate revenue loss. Domain model change touches nearly every bounded context (billing, routing, notifications, tracking, reporting). Rollback complexity is extreme once multi-recipient shipments exist in production.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers (Senders)", "impact": "New capability to ship to multiple recipients. UX changes required in shipment creation flow. Billing transparency needed for multi-recipient pricing." },
      { "group": "Recipients", "impact": "No change for existing single-recipient shipments. New recipients on multi-recipient shipments need independent tracking and notification." },
      { "group": "Drivers / Couriers", "impact": "Route changes — a single shipment may now require multiple stops. Driver app must display per-recipient delivery instructions and collect per-recipient POD." },
      { "group": "Operations / Dispatch", "impact": "New 'Partially Delivered' state requires updated exception workflows. Capacity planning models must account for multi-stop shipments." },
      { "group": "External Partners (API consumers)", "impact": "Must maintain backward compatibility on v1 API. Partners need migration timeline and v2 API documentation. Breaking changes cause immediate integration failures." },
      { "group": "Finance / Billing", "impact": "Pricing model must be defined before launch. Revenue recognition for partially delivered shipments needs accounting rules." },
      { "group": "Customer Service", "impact": "New inquiry types: partial delivery status, per-recipient issues. Scripts and training must be updated." },
      { "group": "Data / Analytics", "impact": "All shipment-level metrics must be re-evaluated. 'Deliveries' vs 'Shipments' distinction becomes critical." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 52,
    "reasoning": "The spec is directionally clear (1:N recipients with delivery preferences) but leaves critical business decisions undefined: pricing model, maximum recipient count, partial-delivery state machine, and the physical nature of multi-recipient shipments (one parcel with multiple stops vs. multiple parcels). These gaps could lead to fundamentally different implementations. The external API compatibility constraint is mentioned but the migration strategy and timeline are unspecified.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "At-least-one-recipient invariant is universal in logistics",
      "Primary Recipient concept is necessary for backward compatibility",
      "Per-recipient delivery preferences are correctly scoped",
      "Data migration must be lossless for 2M existing records",
      "Legacy API backward compatibility is non-negotiable"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Pricing model for multi-recipient shipments is undefined",
      "Physical fulfillment model unclear: is this one parcel making multiple stops, or multiple parcels from one order?",
      "Shipment status state machine for partial delivery is not specified",
      "Maximum recipients per shipment is not defined",
      "Whether 'Delivery Preferences' is an open-ended set or a fixed enum is unspecified"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "Multi-recipient means multiple delivery destinations (not multiple people at the same address receiving the same parcel)",
      "The Shipment aggregate boundary remains — recipients are children of Shipment, not independent aggregates",
      "A Primary Recipient concept is needed even though the spec does not mention it — this is inferred from backward compatibility needs",
      "External partners cannot be broken and must receive a migration window",
      "Existing shipment history must be preserved with full fidelity"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The physical fulfillment model for multi-recipient shipments is not defined. Is this ONE parcel making multiple delivery stops (relay model), or MULTIPLE parcels created from a single order (split-shipment model)? These two models have fundamentally different implications for routing, tracking, billing, and warehouse operations.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Operations / Dispatch", "Drivers / Couriers", "Warehouse", "Finance / Billing"],
      "options": [
        "A: Single parcel, multiple stops — the same physical package is delivered to each recipient in sequence (relay/chain delivery)",
        "B: Multiple parcels from one order — the shipment is split at a hub into sub-parcels, each independently routed",
        "C: Hybrid — the sender chooses per shipment"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B (split into sub-parcels) is the most common model in logistics for multi-recipient. Option A is niche (document relay). Recommend clarifying with product team before implementation."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Pricing model for multi-recipient shipments is not specified. This directly affects revenue and must be defined before the feature can ship.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Finance / Billing", "Customers (Senders)", "Product"],
      "options": [
        "A: Base shipment fee + per-additional-recipient surcharge",
        "B: Fully independent pricing per recipient (effectively N shipments bundled)",
        "C: Flat rate up to N recipients, then per-recipient beyond N"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A is industry standard. Recommend product and finance alignment before implementation begins."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "The set of 'delivery preferences' is not enumerated. Without knowing the preference types, the domain model cannot be accurately designed. Are these structured fields (time window, signature required, safe place) or free-text instructions?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers / Couriers", "Product", "Customers (Senders)"],
      "options": [
        "A: Define a fixed set of preference types now (time window, signature, safe place, leave-with-neighbor)",
        "B: Allow arbitrary key-value preferences (flexible but harder to enforce)",
        "C: Start with a minimal fixed set, extend later"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option C — start with a well-defined minimal set to avoid unbounded complexity."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "The Shipment status state machine needs a 'Partially Delivered' state (or equivalent) that does not exist today. The full set of valid state transitions for multi-recipient shipments has not been defined.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Operations / Dispatch", "Customer Service", "Customers (Senders)", "Data / Analytics"],
      "options": [
        "A: Add 'Partially Delivered' as a top-level shipment status",
        "B: Keep shipment status simple (In Transit / Delivered / Failed) and track delivery status per-recipient only",
        "C: Composite status derived from recipient statuses"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option C — derive shipment status from the aggregate of recipient statuses. This keeps the domain model honest and avoids manual status management."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
